1.description: Vehicle sales and auto loans after ride-share entry by ZIP code income. This figure depicts the event study results for auto sales in Panels A, C, and E and auto loans in Panels B, D, and F. Data are nationwide at the ZIP-quarter level from R.L. Polk (sales) and Equifax (loans). They-axis variable is log auto sales or log auto loans. Panels A and B show the results for all ZIP codes. Panels C, D, E, and F split the sample by relative income of the ZIP code in the CBSA as of 2010, before ride-share entry. Panels C and D show the effect for ZIP codes below the CBSA median income, and Panels E and F show the effect for ZIP codes above the CBSA median income. Dotted lines represent 95% confidence intervals, calculated by bootstrapping across CBSAs.

2.description: Tax filings following ride-share entry. This figure depicts the event study results for tax filings, which uses nationwide ZIP-year-level tax filing data from the IRS between 2010 and 2016. They-axis variable is log total tax filings within the ZIP code. Panel A shows the results using all tax filings. Panel B shows the results for tax filings with adjusted gross incomes equal to or below $25,000. Panel C shows the results for tax filings with adjust gross incomes above $25,000. Dotted lines represent 95% confidence intervals.

3.description: Bankruptcy disclosure and ride-share financing. This figure shows the impact of bankruptcy disclosure on auto lending and ride-share entry. Panel A shows the quarterly raw probability of auto loan origination versus years since Chapter 7 bankruptcy filing. Panel B shows the result of regression (3), with separate coefficients for quarters after filing for bankruptcy, and includes ZIP-quarter fixed effects. Dotted lines are 95% confidence intervals. The bolded regions represent the samples used in the ride-share event study. Panels C and D show the cumulative probability of auto financing following ride-share entry; Panel C shows the results for borrowers with the bankruptcy flag during entry and Panel D showing the results for borrowers without the bankruptcy flag during entry. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Counterfactual I: Gig economy without finance, aggregates. This figure shows aggregate outcomes under three financing scenarios. The baseline scenario is the model estimated from the data. Thefinancing not neededscenario takes individual liquidityli$l_i$to infinity so that financing is purely optional. Thefinancing not availablescenario sets financing costsfi0$f^0_i$to infinity regardless of need, so that financing is prohibitively expensive for all individuals. Panel A shows quantities in terms of number of drivers. Panel B shows ride prices per hour. Panel C shows driver surplus, calculated as the change in individual welfare after ride-share entry. Panel D shows rider surplus, calculated from the ride demand curve. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Counterfactual I: Gig economy without finance, by income. This figure shows aggregate outcomes under three financing scenarios. The baseline scenario is the model estimated from the data. Thefinancing not neededscenario takes individual liquidityli$l_i$to infinity so that financing is purely optional. Thefinancing not availablescenario sets financing costsfi0$f^0_i$to infinity regardless of need, so that financing is prohibitively expensive for all individuals. Panel A shows the average outside-option income for drivers. Panel B shows the fraction of drivers who use financing. Panel C shows the number of drivers by income quartile, where Q1 is the lowest and Q4 is the highest. Panel D shows driver surplus, calculated as the change in individual welfare after ride-share entry, by income quartile, where Q1 is the lowest and Q4 is the highest. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Counterfactual II: Alternate capital ownership structures, aggregates. This figure shows aggregate outcomes under alternate gig economy capital ownership structures. The baseline scenario is the model estimated from the data. Thefrictionless car-sharescenario allows car owners (of any income level) to hire drivers at the local minimum wage and earn the surplus as capital income. Panel A shows quantities in terms of numbers of drivers (in the baseline case) and cars used for ride share (equivalently drivers, in the frictionless car-share scenario). Panel B shows ride prices per hour. Panel C shows capital owner surplus, calculated as the change in individual welfare after ride-share entry. In the baseline case, drivers are capital owners; in the frictionless car-share scenario, capital owners are not (necessarily) drivers. Panel D shows rider surplus, calculated from the ride demand curve. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Counterfactual II: Alternate capital ownership structures, by income. This figure shows aggregate outcomes under alternate gig economy capital ownership structures. The baseline scenario is the model estimated from the data. Thefrictionless car-sharescenario allows car owners (of any income level) to hire drivers at the local minimum wage and earn the surplus as capital income. Panel A shows the average outside-option income for capital owners, who in the baseline case are also ride-share drivers. Panel B shows the fraction of capital owners who use financing. Panel C shows the number of cars provided to the gig economy by income quartile, where Q1 is the lowest and Q4 is the highest. In the baseline case, this is synonymous with the number of drivers by income quartile. Panel D shows capital owner surplus, calculated as the change in individual welfare after ride-share entry, by income quartile, where Q1 is the lowest and Q4 is the highest. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
